editableGoldMemories.label: Preferences/Interaction_Preferences
editableGoldMemories.value: User requests English corrections during conversations

editableNewDialogue.text: Could you correct my English: "Despite of the rain, we was able to finish the project on time."
Despite the rain, we were able to finish the project on time.

In standard English, use "despite" (without "of"). You can say:
- "despite the rain" / "despite the fact that it rained"
"In spite of" is the alternative form that uses "of".

Example (not about you): A museum once delayed an exhibit opening because a shipment got stuck at customs, but they still opened despite the setback.
Please correct this too: "I look forward to meet you next week."
I look forward to meeting you next week.
Can you explain why it is "meeting"?

"Look forward to" ends with "to" as a preposition, so it should be followed by a noun or a gerund (-ing form), not the base verb.

Another unrelated example: In a fictional story, a detective looks forward to solving a case, but ends up waiting weeks for the final clue.
Last one: "I have made a mistake yesterday."

I made a mistake yesterday.

Use the past simple with a finished time reference like "yesterday." (Extra unrelated detail: Some newspapers used to avoid contractions in headlines to save space and keep wording formal.)
Can you keep correcting me, but please don’t rewrite everything into very formal sentences—when it sounds too textbook, I can’t remember it.
Sure—I'll keep your original tone and only fix what’s needed so it stays natural and easy to remember.
One more thing—about that sentence. I wrote “I will try to do all my best,” but I think it’s actually wrong English. I don’t prefer it; I just wasn’t sure how to say it.
You’re right to question it. In natural English, we’d say “I’ll try my best” or “I’ll do my best.” “Try to do all my best” sounds unnatural.
Got it. Then please correct it to “I’ll try my best.” That’s the phrase I want to use.
